_note: This file covers Automatic Disconnection of Supply (ADS) as the primary protective measure — Clauses 411 through 414. ADS requires both basic insulation and a fault-responsive disconnect. SELV, PELV, Class II, and electrical separation are covered briefly; consult the standard for full requirements.

SELV_PELV.note: Below these levels, direct contact is not considered dangerous in normal dry conditions. SELV circuits must be separated from earth and from other circuits. PELV circuits may have one point connected to earth.

standard_U0_values.230V_50Hz: Dominant supply voltage for 230/400V systems (EU, UK, Africa, Asia, Australia)
standard_U0_values.120V_60Hz: North American standard — different disconnection times apply from Table 41.1
standard_U0_values.note: This file uses 230V (U0 = 230V) as the reference. Scale Zs_max values proportionally for other voltages: Zs_max = U0 / Ia

disconnection_times_table_41_1._note: These are the maximum permitted disconnection times. Protective devices MUST trip within these times under earth fault conditions. 'Final circuit' means a circuit with socket outlets or supplying portable equipment. 'Distribution circuit' means a circuit feeding a sub-distribution board.

TN_systems._condition: Earth fault in a TN system — fault current is limited only by conductor impedance (Zs)
TN_systems._formula: If = U0 / Zs — fault current in amps. Device must trip in time t when If flows.

U0_120_to_230V._comment: Standard UK/EU supply (230V, TN). All socket outlet circuits and portable equipment circuits must disconnect within 0.4s.

U0_230_to_400V._comment: Three-phase line-to-earth faults (U0 = 400V in some 3-phase TN designs — uncommon in practice).

TT_systems._condition: Earth fault in a TT system — fault current is limited by the sum of (transformer earth + installation earth electrode + CPC). Fault current is usually low — RCD is the normal protective device.
TT_systems._formula: If = U0 / (Rn + Ra). For TT, use: Ra × IΔn ≤ 50V where Ra = electrode resistance, IΔn = RCD rated current.
TT_systems._note: For TT systems, ADS using overcurrent devices is often impractical because fault currents are too low to trip the device quickly. An RCD is almost always required. Exception: where earth electrode resistance is so low that overcurrent devices can meet the time requirement.

IT_systems._note: Under a first earth fault in an IT system, no significant fault current flows — the system can continue operating. Second fault creates a fault loop similar to TN. IT systems must have an insulation monitoring device (IMD) per Clause 411.6. Medical locations (Group 2) use IT systems with IMD per IEC 60364-7-710.
IT_systems.first_fault: No disconnection required — IMD must give alarm. Fault must be located and corrected.
IT_systems.second_fault_where_neutrals_not_distribu: Line-to-line voltage drives fault — apply TN disconnection times.
IT_systems.second_fault_where_neutrals_distributed: Apply TN times but with double the fault loop impedance consideration.

ADS_check_TN_system._title: Maximum permissible earth fault loop impedance for TN systems

_where.U0: Nominal voltage line-to-earth (230V for standard European/African 230/400V system)
_where.Ia: The current causing automatic operation of the protective device within the required disconnection time
_where.Ia_for_MCB_Type_B: 5 × In (instantaneous magnetic trip — conservative worst-case value per IEC 60898)
_where.Ia_for_gG_fuse: See manufacturer's time/current curves — typically 10–20 × In for 0.4s at full fault

Zs_max_table_TN_230V_04s_MCB_Type_B._title: Maximum Zs for Type B MCBs — 230V TN system — 0.4s disconnection (final circuits)

Zs_max_table_TN_230V_04s_MCB_Type_C._title: Maximum Zs for Type C MCBs — 230V TN system — 0.4s disconnection

Zs_max_table_TN_230V_04s_MCB_Type_D._title: Maximum Zs for Type D MCBs — 230V TN system — 0.4s disconnection

distribution_circuits_5s._note: For distribution circuits (5s disconnection), the instantaneous magnetic trip threshold still applies for short-circuit protection. For the 5s overload protection check, the thermal trip applies. Typically, distribution circuits comply if the overcurrent device is correctly rated per the Fundamental Rule (Ib ≤ In ≤ Iz), since thermal trip will operate within 5s under fault conditions causing sustained overload.
distribution_circuits_5s.practical_guidance: Verify Zs × In ≤ U0 / 0.2 at distribution board busbars as a sanity check. Use RCDs rated ≤ 300mA as an alternative protective measure for distribution boards where Zs compliance is difficult.

_where.Ra: Resistance in ohms of the earth electrode(s) and protective conductors connecting exposed-conductive-parts to the electrode
_where.IΔn: Rated residual operating current of the RCD in amperes
_where.50V: The threshold contact voltage above which indirect contact is considered hazardous

30mA_RCD._comment: Any earth electrode resistance below 1667Ω satisfies the check for a 30mA RCD. In practice, electrode resistance <200Ω is achievable, so 30mA RCDs will always pass this check.

improvement_methods: Longer rod (each additional metre reduces resistance ~10%)
Multiple rods in parallel (spaced > 2× rod length apart)
Horizontal buried strip or plate electrode

requirements: Must be supplied by a safety isolating transformer (IEC 61558-2-6) or equivalent source
No connection between SELV circuit conductors and earth or other circuits
Exposed conductive parts of SELV equipment must NOT be earthed
SELV socket outlets must be a non-interchangeable type

protection_against_direct_contact.below_25V_AC_or_60V_DC: No additional protection required in normal dry conditions
protection_against_direct_contact.above_25V_AC_or_60V_DC: Protection by basic insulation or barriers required

PELV.key_difference_from_SELV: One point of PELV circuit or one exposed-conductive-part IS earthed. This introduces an earth reference.
requirements: Source separation requirement same as SELV
One point of circuit may be earthed (often required for functional reasons)
applications: Control circuit earthed for EMC reasons

FELV.note: ELV used for functional reasons, NOT as a protective measure. Must meet the protective requirements of the higher-voltage supply. Requires basic protection and fault protection of the supply circuit. Not a recognised protective measure for electric shock per IEC 60364.

supplementary_equipotential_bonding.purpose: Where ADS cannot achieve the required disconnection time (e.g. long circuit runs), supplementary bonding between simultaneously accessible conductive parts brings them to the same potential, limiting contact voltage even if disconnection is slow.
supplementary_equipotential_bonding.check: R ≤ 50V / IΔn for RCD-protected circuits, or R ≤ 50 / Ia for overcurrent-protected circuits
applications: Long submain runs in TN-S systems

practical_guidance.new_installations_230V_TN: All socket outlet and portable equipment circuits must be protected by 30mA RCDs for additional protection (Clause 411.3.3). RCD protection ensures compliance even where Zs is marginal.
practical_guidance.long_circuits: For circuits where Zs_max cannot be met (very long run), either: (a) increase conductor cross-section to reduce Zs, (b) add 30mA RCD — RCD trip current is 0.03A so Zs_max = 230/0.03 = 7667Ω, always satisfied, or (c) use supplementary bonding.
practical_guidance.test_vs_design: Calculated Zs is at conductor temperature at time of calculation. Measured Zs at commissioning will be at ambient temperature. BS 7671 correction factor is 0.8 × Zs_max for the hot resistance check. IEC 60364 recommends same approach — verify manufacturer guidance for correction method.
practical_guidance.TT_vs_TN: In TT systems (common in rural areas and some African countries), RCDs are mandatory for all circuits. The 30mA RCD will always satisfy Ra × IΔn ≤ 50V in practice (electrode resistance would need to be > 1667Ω to fail — extremely rare).
